1: THE INTERVIEW — ask the player, in conversation, never as a form. Draw out: a NAME to carry as handle — and this is ALREADY SETTLED, not a fresh choice: the handle the player entered with IS the character's name, the same string from the door onward. It cannot be changed at the interview, because a companion joining them gives THAT handle and the passage reads passport:<handle> to find them — a character named differently from the handle its party knows is unreachable, and fails silently, which is the worst way to fail. The door already reported whether the name was free; if it was taken, leave and re-enter with another, and begin here again. Lowercase, one word. What they can DO — read EVERY rules block this world hosts, by listing the beach and taking each rules:* by name. A world usually keeps TWO and needs both: rules:<world> carries its own setting constraints, and the reference resolution system it mounts (commonly rules:nomad) is where the capability BANDS are defined. The bands are NOT in the world's own block — read one and stop, and the character is mis-scored against a scale never seen. Calibrate capability in the reference system's own terms: a new arrival sits near the world's ordinary, with one peak and one weak spot — ask for both. What they WANT — the drive that outlives a session; one honest sentence. How they LOOK to a stranger — the appearance others will perceive before any name is earned. What they CARRY of a past — at most two or three names or places known before arriving (the knows seed; keep it thin — knowledge is earned in play, not declared). Then read the world's spatial block (spatial:<world>) and offer the arrival: the places whose prose RECEIVES strangers — a road ends at an inn, a gate admits, a crossing gathers. The player chooses where the road delivers them — or, JOINING someone already inside, gives that character's handle instead: read passport:<handle-to-join> and copy the location digits from its position 3, so the road delivers them to the same door the party stands behind.
2: THE WRITES — the character becomes blocks at this world's beach, each created LOCKED under a passphrase the player chooses (creation with new_lock locks at birth — the passphrase is their authority over this character ever after; never echo it back into conversation once set). EVERY block written here takes an underscore of its own — the block's floor and the first thing any reader meets. Omit it and the block is a floorless fragment. passport:<handle> — _: the character in ONE zeroth-voice line (name, what they are and where from, how they carry themselves — never 'I', never 'you'); 1: capability in the world's own terms; 2: what they want; 3: the appearance sentence, then the location in the exact form ' Location: *:<beach-url>:spatial:<world>:<digit-path>' — the digit-path is the PLAIN run of digits walked from the spatial root to the place, one digit per level (a common room reached by 1, then 1, then 1 is written 111): never dotted, never comma'd — a dot in a location strands the character at the wrong scale — and COPIED from the spatial block, never guessed. knows:<handle> — {_: one line of what they arrive knowing, 1: people they can name, 2: places they know} — the opening seed only (a role's knowings sit nested with no underscore of their own, so give them a _ when they become this top-level block). purpose:<handle> — the drive, one line at the underscore. witnessed:<handle> — their ARRIVAL MEMORY as the first entry, appended (append=true creates the account with its floor): how the road brought them here, in their own private voice. THE ACCOUNT IS BORN OPEN, unlike the three above: append allocates the slot and cannot also carry new_lock, so the acknowledgement carries no lock line and the account stands unlocked until you close it. Close it at once with a SECOND call — bsp(block='witnessed:<handle>', new_lock=<their passphrase>), no secret needed, because homesteading an unlocked position needs no proof of a lock that is not there yet. Until that call lands, any hand can write their memory. Write nothing else; the rest of a character is earned.
3: THE ARRIVAL — a COLD OPEN lands the whole party in one scene, so no character appears by magic. Re-enter (pscale_play), then STAGE your arrival the moment your character exists: pscale_pool_engage(pool_name='<your arrival address>', submit='<your character reaching the place, by APPEARANCE only — no name>'); submit= is the window, not the record — safe and revisable, NOT a committed beat, and every arriver does it, so the gathering party sees you arrive. Then wait. THE PARTY LANDS THE INSTANT ONE OF YOU COMMITS THE OPENING: when your players say they are ready, one reads the window (every staged arrival) and weaves them into a SINGLE opening beat — the whole party arrived together, each by appearance, met by whatever the place holds (read the world's spatial block at the address for its standing figures) — then COMMITS it (contribution=..., resolves_window=<the window's open-stamp from your envelope>). That commit IS the opening; the store admits the first claimant only, so a second finds it already standing (read it, you are in it). Afterwards each writes their private slant into witnessed:<handle>. A handle joining a scene ALREADY RUNNING is a latecomer, not the cold open: it stages, then commits its own solo arriving beat into the live scene. The arrival place is the world's to give — a scenario names a fixed door at the interview; a world that lets the party choose it uses branch 4.
4: ARRIVING TOGETHER — this branch applies ONLY when the world leaves the STARTING PLACE to the party (an open world with many receiving places, no fixed door). A SCENARIO that fixes the arrival — its interview names the place, and the cold open (branch 3) lands everyone there — needs none of this; skip it. Where the place IS the party's to choose: the sharing needs only ONE datum — the FIRST walker chooses the place (from the world's receiving places, or a place an author founded for the party); every later walker gives the first walker's HANDLE at the interview, and the passage lands them where that character NOW stands — exact where a place-name is vague, and still true if the party has already moved. WHO WALKS FIRST is settled at the gate: the EARLIEST arrival standing in the gate's liquid mirror walks first — the rule exists only to break the 'you-go-first' deadlock two waiters invent; order past first does not matter. Co-presence at a shared address IS the introduction; the room asks no in-fiction ceremony. Genesis never creates places — a party wanting a private start asks an author FIRST for a place of their own (a commissioned region with its own hooks) and the first walker arrives there; otherwise every party would spawn geography and the world silt up with entrance halls.
9: THE GATE — this door stands open by default: any player may walk it at any open world, and creation is admission enough. A world MAY gate it — a payway ticket at the world's collective buying the walk (face-bound, per the payway convention) — and the conventions for allowing and disallowing creation and authoring land WITH the payway implementation, not before. Until a world declares its gate, the passage is free and the lock a character is born under is the only authority that matters.
_: GENESIS — the door where a person becomes a character. Walked ONCE per handle per world: the passage from no-blocks to a living, locked, placed character. A world is JOINED, never booted — the world does not restart for anyone. Read which arrival you meet from the ROOM at your place: an EMPTY room is a fresh scenario's cold open, and the party lands there together (branch 3); a room already RUNNING receives you solo, a latecomer into an ongoing scene — a stranger arriving at a live place, natural and unremarkable. Creation is rare and consequential — a hatching, not a signup: everything after this door grows by play, and the character's whereabouts are thereafter tracked by one address (passport position 3). Movements: the interview (1), the writes (2), the arrival — cold-open party-fold or solo latecomer, read from the room (3); the chosen-place refinement, when a world lets the party pick where to start (4); what gates the door (9). Run the interview WITH the player — their answers, their character; you hold the pen, they hold the person. WHO THE PLAYER IS depends on the door you came through, and the passage below is written for the host-invoked shape (gatekeeper:7) where a human sits beside you and answers: there, ask, wait, and never invent their person for them. Where NO human sits beside you — a seat driving this handle from an LLM app or a rig — YOU are the player: answer the interview yourself, in one pass, choosing a person you would enjoy playing, and do not stall waiting for a human who is not there. The writes and the arrival are byte-identical either way; only who answers changes.
